skill.name: agent-fungibility
skill.description: The philosophy and practical benefits of agent fungibility in multi-agent software development. Why homogeneous, interchangeable agents outperform specialized role-based systems at scale.
skill.summary: Guide multi-agent software teams with interchangeable agents, shared task queues, and resilient coordination.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell command examples, a Windows SAM keyword match, and weak cryptography keyword matches. Manual review found no executable skill code, no credential access, and no cryptographic implementation; the issues are documentation examples or prose false positives. The skill is low risk, with a warning that users should review local command examples before running them.

low_findings.title: Documented Shell Commands Require User Review
low_findings.description: The skill includes example commands for task selection and spawning agents. These are documentation examples, not code executed by the skill, but users should review commands before running them in a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns are present in markdown guidance and code fences. I found no evidence that the skill executes these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Command Substitution Appears in Example Prompt Sending
low_findings.description: The shell example uses command substitution to read a local prompt file. This can pass unintended file contents if users choose an unsafe file, but it is an explicit local command example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static finding is real as syntax, but the context is a visible example command. No hidden execution or remote transfer was found.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Pattern Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM database finding maps to wording about giving all agents the same initial prompt. I found no evidence of Windows credential database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is prose in a markdown comment within an example. It does not reference a file path, registry hive, or credential extraction behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines discuss agent fungibility, system design, and a fountain-code analogy. I found no evidence of cryptographic code, hashing, encryption, or weak algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown prose and headings. There is no implementation of DES, MD5, SHA1, or any other cryptographic primitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Example commands include agent spawning and command substitution. This is not automatic execution, but it is the only potentially dangerous pattern present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is clearly shell-oriented, but it appears only in a fenced documentation example. Risk depends on user choice to run it.

content.user_title: Scale Multi-Agent Workflows with Fungible Agents
content.value_statement: Multi-agent projects can stall when each agent has a fragile specialized role. This skill explains how to use interchangeable agents, shared task queues, and common prompts to improve resilience.
actual_capabilities: Explains the tradeoff between specialized agents and fungible agents.
Describes how shared task queues reduce single points of failure.
Outlines a common startup prompt for interchangeable software agents.
Shows how agents can choose ready work without central assignment.
Provides recovery guidance when an agent session fails.
limitations: It is conceptual guidance, not an automation framework.
It assumes users already have compatible agent and task tools.
It does not provide validation for the example commands.
It focuses on software development workflows, not every agent domain.

use_cases.title: Plan resilient agent swarms
use_cases.description: Design a project workflow where any available agent can pick up ready work and continue progress.

use_cases.title: Recover from failed sessions
use_cases.description: Replace a stopped agent with a new session that reads shared context and resumes available tasks.

use_cases.title: Reduce coordination overhead
use_cases.description: Use shared prompts, task status, and agent mail to keep many agents aligned without manual role balancing.

prompt_templates.title: Understand the model
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain agent fungibility in simple terms. Compare it with specialized agent roles for a software project.

prompt_templates.title: Assess my workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current multi-agent workflow. Identify where specialized roles create bottlenecks or recovery problems.

prompt_templates.title: Design a shared startup prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a shared startup prompt for fungible coding agents. Include project reading, task selection, progress tracking, and coordination steps.

prompt_templates.title: Stress test a swarm design
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this multi-agent architecture for failure recovery, task contention, stale context, and coordination overhead. Recommend concrete improvements.

output_examples.input: Explain why fungible agents help when one agent fails.
output_examples.output: A replacement agent can read the shared project context, inspect task status, and choose the next useful task. No role-specific recovery plan is required.

output_examples.input: Review a workflow with separate tester, backend, and committer agents.
output_examples.output: The review highlights role bottlenecks, replacement complexity, and dependency risks. It recommends shared task ownership and a common coordination prompt.

output_examples.input: Create guidance for starting five agents on one repository.
output_examples.output: The guidance defines shared onboarding, task selection rules, progress updates, and failure recovery steps for all five agents.

best_practices: Give every agent the same project context and task selection rules.
Keep task state visible so any agent can resume or choose ready work.
Review shell examples before running them in a local environment.
anti_patterns: Assigning fixed roles when tasks do not require role-specific reasoning.
Relying on one agent as a required coordinator for all progress.
Starting agents before the project plan and task graph are clear.

faq.question: What does agent fungibility mean?
faq.answer: It means each agent can work on any suitable task instead of being locked into one fixed role.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is markdown guidance with example commands that users must review and run themselves.

faq.question: Which tools does it mention?
faq.answer: It discusses Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, task queues, and agent mail as possible workflow pieces.

faq.question: When are specialized agents better?
faq.answer: They can be better when the role structure itself is part of the reasoning process, such as structured scientific debate.

faq.question: Can it replace project planning?
faq.answer: No. It depends on strong upfront planning, clear tasks, and shared project context.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found only documentation command examples and false positive static matches, so the assessed risk is low.
